lead: A living, open archive of the dance, music, people and communities that carried Jumpstyle from Belgian clubs to a worldwide digital culture.

timeline.title: The sound begins to take shape
timeline.text: A slower, bouncier alternative develops between Belgian club culture and Dutch hardcore. Speedy J's Pull Over becomes one of the era's defining records.
timeline.impact: It establishes the musical foundation from which Belgian and Dutch Jump would develop into a distinct scene.

timeline.title: Poing turns a raw club sound into a national hit
timeline.text: Rotterdam Termination Source reaches number two in the Dutch Top 40 and stays there for four weeks, proving that uncompromising harder dance music could cross into national pop culture.
timeline.impact: Its chart breakthrough proved that the emerging sound could travel from underground clubs into mass culture.

timeline.title: Belgian labels and clubs build a home
timeline.text: Bonzai Jumps separates the emerging sound from hardcore while venues such as Complexe Cap'tain anchor the scene in Belgium and northern France.
timeline.impact: A stable ecosystem of venues and record labels gave the sound the infrastructure needed to survive, circulate and evolve.

timeline.title: Hakken establishes the movement vocabulary around hardcore
timeline.text: A surviving Dutch documentary preserves hakken as the dominant physical language of gabber culture, the scene against which Jump's slower stamping identity would develop.
timeline.impact: Hakken supplied a recognizable footwork vocabulary that later Jumpstyle dancers could adapt, contrast with and build upon.

timeline.title: Jumper Records names a movement
timeline.text: Da Rick, Da Boy Tommy and their circle combine retro sounds with harder kicks. Jumper Records launches as a dedicated home for the new Belgian sound called Jump.
timeline.impact: The label helped consolidate 'Jump' as a public identity and created a durable institution linking artists, records and audiences.

timeline.title: Skïen gives Jump music its own dance
timeline.text: The ski-like dragging and jumping base associated with Cherry Moon becomes the common ancestor from which Oldschool, Hardjump and Tekstyle distinctions later emerge.
timeline.impact: This club-born dance separated Jumpstyle's movement language from adjacent rave practices and made participation central to the culture.

timeline.title: Halloween becomes a Belgian hit
timeline.text: Da Boy Tommy's Halloween enters the official Ultratop and sells more than 45,000 copies in Flanders, carrying Jump into the charts.
timeline.impact: The hit widened public recognition of the sound and confirmed that Belgian Jump could sustain mainstream attention.

timeline.title: Paris club nights create a cross-border laboratory
timeline.text: Tecktonik Killer nights program Belgian Jump and Dutch Hardstyle together near Paris, accelerating the exchange that later supports French Tekstyle and electro culture.
timeline.impact: Regular exchange between French dancers and Belgian music culture made Jumpstyle cross-border before online video accelerated its spread.

timeline.title: Schoolyard footage preserves the club-to-web bridge
timeline.text: Early footage from the Lokeren and Sint-Niklaas orbit captures dancers outside a school, close to clubs such as Cherry Moon, The Stars and Complex.
timeline.impact: The footage captures the decisive transition from local imitation to shareable online practice, making the dance reproducible beyond clubs.

timeline.title: Tutorials turn viewers into jumpers
timeline.text: Dion Teurlings' early public videos and JumpingJob's tutorials show how Google Video and YouTube transformed a local dance into something anyone could learn.
timeline.impact: Tutorials converted passive viewers into practitioners and made technique transferable without geographic access to an established scene.

timeline.title: My own jumpstyle signals individual style online
timeline.text: MarjinV's upload preserves an early use of Own and Jumpstyle together, anticipating the language of personal technique and identity that would define the competitive era.
timeline.impact: The phrase documents an early shift from copying a shared basic to treating personal variation as a legitimate creative value.

timeline.title: Dirty Workz builds a platform for the next hard-dance generation
timeline.text: The Belgian label gives Coone and later harder-dance artists an international platform closely connected to the music used by Jumpstyle's web generation.
timeline.impact: The label created long-term infrastructure for music that fueled Jumpstyle training, videos and crossover with the wider hard-dance ecosystem.

timeline.title: Jump Is The Style becomes an early learning hub
timeline.text: Demonstration videos and a dedicated tutorial-and-forum site turn isolated uploads into repeatable knowledge that newcomers around the world can learn and discuss.
timeline.impact: A centralized learning resource helped standardize vocabulary and lower the entry barrier for the first internet-native generation.

timeline.title: Jumping Is Not a Crime becomes a community identity
timeline.text: The slogan moves from skate culture into Jumpstyle videos and later commercial compilations, giving a decentralized youth scene a shared declaration.
timeline.impact: The slogan transformed a dance preference into a visible collective identity that participants could repeat across borders.

timeline.title: Jumpstyle reaches the top of Dutch pop culture
timeline.text: Jeckyll & Hyde's Freefall reaches number one in the Dutch Top 40, while Patrick Jumpen's Holiday peaks at number five.
timeline.impact: Chart success made dancers, music and visual codes legible to a national mass audience at the height of the first wave.

timeline.title: 14,000 people attend a Dutch jump festival
timeline.text: Patrick Jumpen opens Herfstpop in Rijswijk with a jump contest and Jeckyll & Hyde also perform, documenting the scale of the youth phenomenon.
timeline.impact: The scale demonstrated that Jumpstyle was not only an online trend but a mass participatory culture capable of sustaining major live events.

timeline.title: Berlin meetings make the multijump monumental
timeline.text: Large German gatherings place synchronized multijumps at the center of the public meeting culture and help the dance travel through YouTube.
timeline.impact: Large synchronized meetings established the multijump as a defining community ritual and a powerful format for online circulation.

timeline.title: Scooter gives Jumpstyle a global pop vehicle
timeline.text: Jumping All Over the World pairs harder-dance music with Jumpstyle-oriented choreography and carries the movement far beyond its continental club origins.
timeline.impact: An internationally established act carried Jumpstyle references into markets far beyond its Belgian-Dutch core.

timeline.title: The First Russian Jumpstyle League is online
timeline.text: A surviving league preview and an archived Jumpstylers.ru front page preserve freestyle brackets, semifinals and a division for new jumpers.
timeline.impact: The league showed that competition could organize a distant national scene online and helped make Russia a major center of technical development.

timeline.title: Poland gathers at national scale
timeline.text: A major Krakow meeting records the strength of Poland's meeting culture as new tricks, spins and the Ownstyle identity take shape.
timeline.impact: National-scale meetings connected city scenes, strengthened a shared Polish identity and created one of Jumpstyle's most durable communities.

timeline.title: French electro meets Belgian Jump
timeline.text: Le Monde documents French club nights mixing Belgian Jumpstyle, Dutch Hardstyle, minimal electro and Tekhouse, a key cross-border exchange in the French scene.
timeline.impact: The crossover expanded the dance's musical possibilities and connected Jumpstyle with the broader late-2000s electro movement.

timeline.title: Germany institutionalizes Jumpstyle competition
timeline.text: The first TAF national championship gathers more than 160 participants across solo, duo and group divisions, moving the dance into a formal national competition system.
timeline.impact: A national championship moved competition from informal exchanges toward repeatable rules, public recognition and institutional scale.

timeline.title: A Jumpstyle album reaches number one in the United Kingdom
timeline.text: Scooter's album spends seven weeks in the UK top ten and 21 weeks in the top 100, the clearest Jumpstyle-linked album crossover outside continental Europe.
timeline.impact: Reaching the top of a major market demonstrated Jumpstyle's international commercial reach beyond continental club culture.

timeline.title: Poland records an official nationwide meeting
timeline.text: The Poznań film explicitly presents the gathering as Poland's first official national meeting, consolidating the collective structure behind Ownstyle's rise.
timeline.impact: The event formalized coordination between regional scenes and left a shared national reference for later generations.

timeline.title: JumpstylePolska connects meetings and leagues
timeline.text: A Wayback snapshot preserves dedicated forum sections for Zloty and Liga Jumpstyle Polska, evidence of the infrastructure behind Poland's national scene.
timeline.impact: The platform linked information, competition and gatherings, giving the Polish scene durable connective infrastructure.

timeline.title: A footwear campaign backs a national meeting
timeline.text: Olympikus works with the Orkut-era Jumpstyle Brasil community on a major national meeting, showing an early bridge between an online dance community and a Brazilian brand.
timeline.impact: Commercial sponsorship documented Jumpstyle's visibility outside its own channels and enabled a community gathering at national scale.

timeline.title: Berlin connects a national meeting network
timeline.text: Roughly 200 dancers from several German cities combine a mass jump and crew competition at the central station and Lustgarten during the sixth Berlin meeting.
timeline.impact: The sixth meeting demonstrates continuity: repeat gatherings turned isolated viral moments into an enduring German network.

timeline.title: 1,067 dancers set a Jumpstyle world record
timeline.text: Bielefeld's thirteen-minute mass dance receives Guinness recognition and creates one of the clearest public measurements of Jumpstyle's peak participation.
timeline.impact: The record made mass participation globally newsworthy and remains one of the clearest numerical demonstrations of the dance's peak reach.

timeline.title: Stamp on the Ground becomes a lasting crossover anthem
timeline.text: ItaloBrothers turn stamping language and choreography into an internationally durable pop reference that continues introducing new audiences to Jumpstyle-adjacent movement.
timeline.impact: Its video and refrain became a durable entry point whose association with Jumpstyle continued long after the first mainstream wave.

timeline.title: A French competitive generation develops
timeline.text: Spiady and Rey'Steal become international references for French style, spins and technical interpretation during the league era.
timeline.impact: French leagues advanced technical exchange and helped establish France as a source of influential competitive dancers rather than only an adopter.

timeline.title: An iconic Russian multijump crosses borders
timeline.text: Russian jumpers gather in a dance studio to Noisecontrollers' Confucius. The recording becomes one of the scene's enduring collective videos.
timeline.impact: The video made Russia's scale and technical identity visible internationally, inspiring imitation and strengthening East-West exchange.

timeline.title: A Women's World Jumpstyle League creates a dedicated division
timeline.text: The international competition ends with Alinka as champion, preserving a rare formal space centered on women inside the league-driven culture.
timeline.impact: A dedicated women's league created visibility and competitive space in a scene whose most circulated rankings were overwhelmingly male.

timeline.title: Generations meet in Poznan
timeline.text: Patrick Jumpen and Dion Teurlings visit a Polish meeting, connecting the Dutch web-explosion generation with Poland's competitive community.
timeline.impact: The gathering preserved intergenerational transmission, allowing experience, style vocabulary and community memory to move between waves.

timeline.title: Ukraine's complexity generation emerges
timeline.text: Woren, later known as Anointed, is followed by J*Air, Belir and organizer Shkap as Ukrainian jumpers gain international recognition.
timeline.impact: Ukrainian dancers pushed complexity and control forward, broadening the international standard of what advanced Jumpstyle could look like.

timeline.title: Dany carries Italy into the international canon
timeline.text: Dany becomes one of the names associated with originality and innovation in the modern competitive era.
timeline.impact: Dany's visibility connected the Italian scene to international competition and gave local dancers a widely recognized reference.

timeline.title: Walle becomes a Mexican reference
timeline.text: Walle's powerful style makes Mexico visible in the international competitive community and inspires a later national generation.
timeline.impact: Walle's recognition made Latin American technical contribution visible within a history often narrated mainly through Europe and Russia.

timeline.title: Lauth defines a Chilean presence
timeline.text: Lauth becomes one of South America's internationally recognized jumpers and a bridge between Chile and other national communities.
timeline.impact: Lauth gave Chile a durable point of identification in the international network and widened the map of recognized Jumpstyle practice.

timeline.title: Cosmic represents Indonesia's technical wave
timeline.text: Cosmic becomes a key international reference from an Indonesian community known for rhythm, speed and technical ambition.
timeline.impact: Cosmic's visibility showed that high-level Jumpstyle had become established in Southeast Asia, not merely consumed there.

timeline.title: The HEB league expands competitive formats
timeline.text: Six months of solo, duo, Hardjump, women's and team divisions show how online leagues had matured into specialized international competition ecosystems.
timeline.impact: Its diversified format tested new ways to compare style and technique, keeping online competition creatively productive after the first boom.

timeline.title: Los Angeles holds its sixth annual meeting
timeline.text: The title and archived page preserve a sixth annual Los Angeles Jump Meeting and the Southern California crews that participated.
timeline.impact: Six consecutive editions demonstrate sustained community organization in North America despite distance from Jumpstyle's European center.

timeline.title: Belgium loses pioneer Da Boy Tommy
timeline.text: Tommy Debie dies at 36 after a road accident. His catalogue remains a foundation of Jump's first commercial era.
timeline.impact: The community response consolidated historical memory around a foundational producer and underscored the need to preserve Jumpstyle's first generation.

timeline.title: Indonesia demonstrates a coordinated national scene
timeline.text: A national meeting at Gelora Bung Karno in Jakarta documents sustained collective organization beyond Jumpstyle's European and Latin American centers.
timeline.impact: A national meeting converted dispersed online participation into visible local infrastructure and collective identity.

timeline.title: Fireborn Squad connects Brazilian generations
timeline.text: The national team is formed to unite leading jumpers from different regions and eras and represent Brazil in international leagues.
timeline.impact: The crew maintained training, identity and mentorship across different Brazilian waves, creating continuity beyond isolated viral moments.

timeline.title: To Da Bone carries internet Jumpstyle onto the contemporary stage
timeline.text: (LA)HORDE premieres a work with eleven jumpers recruited across Europe, translating an online-native dance community into major international contemporary-dance institutions.
timeline.impact: The work reframed internet-born Jumpstyle as contemporary performance art and brought its community history to international theatre audiences.

timeline.title: Jumpstyle-derived movement enters Fortnite
timeline.text: The Springy emote, internally identified as EID_JumpStyleDance, carries the movement vocabulary into one of the world's largest games.
timeline.impact: Inclusion in a globally distributed game exposed Jumpstyle-derived movement to millions, even as attribution and cultural context became contested.

timeline.title: Yabujin opens an unexpected path back to Jumpstyle
timeline.text: Yabujin and the DJ GYROTTA ZAO alias combine jumpstyle music with a distinct internet mythology, leading a new audience toward the dance through TikTok edits.
timeline.impact: The artist's internet mythology introduced Jumpstyle sounds and imagery to a younger underground audience, helping seed the current revival.

timeline.title: Jumpstyle United Nations is founded
timeline.text: Jumpers, organizers, DJs and archivists from multiple generations and nations begin an open repository for timelines, knowledge, community mapping and preservation.
timeline.impact: A shared open archive turns fragmented national memory into maintainable public infrastructure and makes historical correction a collaborative process.

timeline.title: Warsaw records a new-generation multijump
timeline.text: A large meeting in Warsaw brings Polish jumpers together again, preserving the country's collective tradition for a new audience.
timeline.impact: The meeting demonstrates living intergenerational continuity in one of the culture's strongest national scenes, not only nostalgia for its peak.

timeline.title: Jumper Records returns
timeline.text: Da Rick revives the Belgian label with new releases, events and an active catalogue, reconnecting the 1997 name with today's global audience.
timeline.impact: Reviving a foundational label reconnects today's releases and events to the institution that helped name and distribute the culture in the 1990s.

timeline.title: TOPJUMPERZ returns as a worldwide distributed league
timeline.text: Badsz, a Russian jumper and founder of Russian Village Boys, reconnects his historic TOPJUMPERZ project with a global competition based on country representation, participant uploads under a shared title pattern, structured judging and a €2,000 prize pool.
timeline.impact: Its common title format lets entrants publish from their own channels while country representation, judging and prizes reconnect decentralized creators to a shared global event; its long-term impact is still emerging.

figures.name: Patrick Jumpen
figures.note: The project that brought tutorials, performances and Jumpstyle videos to a global mass audience.

figures.name: Jeckyll & Hyde (Maarten Vorwerk)
figures.note: Maarten Vorwerk's Jumpstyle project behind "Freefall", the scene's defining Dutch number-one single, and the charting follow-up "Frozen Flame".

figures.name: DannieL
figures.note: A defining Ownstyle reference during Poland's rise as a technical and competitive center.

figures.name: Samurai
figures.note: An early Russian icon associated with Sidejump, power and the first league generation.

figures.name: Rumun
figures.note: An early JumpstylePolska figure and league finalist against Cebul, also known as Freakaz for music made for the community.

figures.name: Yarche
figures.note: An early Russian Freestyle reference, first-league-era name and author-presenter of preserved Hardjump training material for JumpStylers.ru.

figures.name: Cebul
figures.note: The first jumper recorded as gaining prominence through JumpstylePolska, later preserved in league finals and duo competition.

figures.name: Chruściel
figures.note: A Polish league-era competitor preserved in the fifth edition of the JumpstylePolska league, representing Ownstyle's organized competitive wave.

figures.name: Xardas
figures.note: A leading figure in the high-complexity Polish evolution era.

figures.name: Spiady
figures.note: A key French figure associated with style, technical structure and HSA.

figures.name: Badsz
figures.note: A Russian reference whose videos and compilations helped define a generation.

figures.name: Effect
figures.note: One of Poland's widely cited technical figures from the league era.

figures.name: Rey’Steal
figures.note: A French reference remembered for style, spinning and competitive influence.

figures.name: Jagr
figures.note: A prominent Russian jumper in the international complexity era.

figures.name: Groszek
figures.note: A Polish technical-generation figure from around 2010 whose surviving archive includes a group round in the Jumpstyle.boo.pl league.

figures.name: DudQ
figures.note: A Polish 2010-era figure whose collaborative film with Xardas, "Time of Death", preserves the country's high-complexity Ownstyle period.

figures.name: Cost
figures.note: A Russian Sidejump-era competitor who won the 2011 Russian New Generation division and also recorded cross-jumper feature films.

figures.name: Dany
figures.note: An Italian figure recognized for originality and innovation.

figures.name: Walle
figures.note: A powerful Mexican reference who made the country visible internationally.

figures.name: Lukier
figures.note: Part of the modern Polish generation that continued advancing technique.

figures.name: Lauth
figures.note: A central Chilean figure and an active bridge between South American and European scenes.

figures.name: Cosmic
figures.note: An Indonesian reference associated with rhythm and technical ambition.

figures.name: Sosho
figures.note: A modern Polish influence whose technique and visual identity traveled worldwide.

figures.name: Phantom
figures.note: A modern-era Polish figure with lasting international visibility.

figures.name: Gigi
figures.note: Named by the 2025 community review among Poland's visible 2008 figures during the formative Ownstyle and online-league wave; a stable individual video is still being researched.

figures.name: Hyper-X
figures.note: An early Polish league competitor preserved winning a duo quarterfinal with Browar in the fourth JumpstylePolska league edition.

figures.name: Kamilkadze
figures.note: A Polish international-league competitor preserved through World Jumpstyle League rounds and identified with Wiewior among the leaders of a Czech-hosted league.

figures.name: Wiewior
figures.note: Winner of the ninth JumpstylePolska league final against Kamill and an international competitor also associated with a Czech-hosted league.

figures.name: Philip
figures.note: Polish winner of the 2021 World Jumpstyle League Gold Division, defeating DejuX in the final after a documented selection-to-title campaign.

figures.name: Styl3dd
figures.note: A Dutch early-online jumper recorded as emerging in 2007 and publishing by 2008, with surviving solo films and a collaboration with Russia's Brik.

figures.name: Jelle
figures.note: A Dutch jumper who began learning from online videos around 2007, started publishing in 2008 and later gained renewed visibility through the Jumpers Versus Project.

figures.name: Brik
figures.note: An early Russian figure from the 2008 wave, preserved in International Jumpstyle League Freestyle competition and a cross-border feature with Styl3dd.

figures.name: Scot
figures.note: A standout Russian figure of the 2010 generation, preserved winning multiple World Jumpstyle League rounds and competing in duo championships with Pentalgin.

figures.name: Woren (now Anointed)
figures.note: A Ukrainian complexity-era figure active by 2010, preserved in the Ukraine JumpStyle Legion's Premier division before later using the name Anointed.

figures.name: J*Air
figures.note: A Ukrainian league figure with preserved Ukraine JumpStyle Legion wins against Shkap, Woren and Pappy, followed by World Jumpstyle League participation.

figures.name: Belir
figures.note: A Ukrainian competitor active in the Ukraine JumpStyle Legion who reached the 2012 World Summer Jumpstyle League final against Vanito.

figures.name: Shkap
figures.note: A prominent Ukrainian organizer and competitor, preserved reaching a national Freestyle semifinal and remaining active in international leagues through WJL 2014.

figures.name: Mr. Covin
figures.note: A French jumper with documented longevity from a 2010 feature with Bew-C-Tyl to later French leagues, including a French Cup 2020 semifinal win.

figures.name: RafaJumpen
figures.note: An old-school Brazilian reference who preserved the 2011 Ibirapuera meeting, recorded an early duo with IvanJumper and later rejoined the FBS generation.

figures.name: Matrix
figures.note: A founding Fireborn Squad member, Brazilian league representative and SIJL semifinalist against Jelle who also joined the 2023 Brazil-Chile exchange.

figures.name: Cursebird
figures.note: A founding Fireborn Squad member, finalist in the sixth Jumpstyle Brasil league edition and a continuing Brazilian representative in international leagues.

figures.name: Mreaggle
figures.note: Organizer, jumper and founder of JUN, focused on reconnecting generations and preserving global history.

figures.name: LuFe
figures.note: A founding Fireborn Squad member whose archive includes a JumpBattle win over Cursebird and community tutorials focused on tricks and combinations.

figures.name: SonnyJumpen
figures.note: A German jumper whose surviving solo archive explicitly combines Hardjump and Tekstyle, alongside duo features from the country's meeting era.

figures.name: Rebel-S
figures.note: A German jumper remembered through "The F-Jump", a solo record of the individualized style-building that followed the first mass-meeting wave.

figures.name: Owen
figures.note: A German Ownstyle jumper whose preserved "My Time" short story presents the dance through a personal, narrative solo format.

figures.name: Linda
figures.note: A German female jumper whose 2013 three-year anniversary film preserves women's participation in the scene beyond dedicated league divisions.

figures.name: Ducci
figures.note: A German jumper associated with Jumpstyle Crew Uckermark, preserving the regional crew structure that sustained the national scene.

figures.name: Marshall
figures.note: A Mexican competitor preserved representing the country in World Jumpstyle League group competition, alongside a personal practice archive.

figures.name: Kada
figures.note: A Chilean jumper preserved in the "Superstars" collaboration with Lauth and Frannkz, documenting the country's collective style identity.
